intro_text: Hi there! 😊 Today we master Statistics — the science of collecting, organising and interpreting data! From frequency tables to histograms to mean, median and mode — these tools help us make sense of the world! 🎯
complete_message: You have completed ALL 12 chapters of Class 9 Mathematics! Statistics is now yours 💪

explain_questions.question: What is Statistics and what are the key terms?
explain_questions.question_spoken: What is Statistics and what are the key terms?

steps.t: STATISTICS is the science of collecting, organising, presenting, analysing and interpreting numerical data to draw conclusions. DATA = collection of numbers/facts gathered for analysis.
steps.s: Statistics is the science of collecting, organising and interpreting data. Data is the collection of numbers gathered for analysis.

steps.t: Key terms: PRIMARY DATA = collected directly by the investigator (surveys, experiments). SECONDARY DATA = collected by someone else (newspapers, government records). RAW DATA = data in original unorganised form.
steps.s: Primary data is collected directly. Secondary data is collected by others. Raw data is unorganised original data.

steps.t: POPULATION = the entire group being studied. SAMPLE = a smaller group chosen from the population. VARIABLE = the characteristic being measured. OBSERVATION = each value in the data. FREQUENCY = how many times a value occurs.
steps.s: Population is the whole group. Sample is a part of it. Frequency is how many times a value occurs.

explain_questions.confirm_question: Data collected by the investigator directly is called ______ data.
explain_questions.confirm_question_spoken: Data collected by the investigator directly is called blank data.
nudges: There are two types: primary and secondary
If YOU collect the data yourself through surveys or experiments...
Answer: Primary data
explain_questions.anim_svg: <g id="st1s0" opacity="0"><rect x="10" y="8" width="390" height="30" rx="7" fill="#f5e6c8"/><text x="210" y="28" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#d4870a">Statistics: Collect→Organise→Present→Analyse→Interpret</text></g><g id="st1s1" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="68" font-size="11" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Primary: collected directly | Secondary: from others</text></g><g id="st1s2" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="108" font-size="11" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Population: whole group | Sample: part | Frequency: count</text></g><g id="st1ans" opacity="0"><rect x="40" y="148" width="320" height="24" rx="7" fill="#eef2eb" stroke="#6b4c2a" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="210" y="163" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Share Tech Mono" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#7a8c6e">Collected directly = Primary data</text></g>

explain_questions.question: What is a Frequency Distribution Table?
explain_questions.question_spoken: What is a Frequency Distribution Table?

steps.t: A FREQUENCY DISTRIBUTION TABLE organises raw data into classes (groups) and shows how many observations fall in each class. It makes large data easy to understand at a glance!
steps.s: A frequency distribution table groups data into classes and shows the count in each group.

steps.t: Terms: CLASS INTERVAL = the range of each group (e.g. 0-10, 10-20). CLASS WIDTH = upper limit − lower limit (=10 in this case). CLASS MARK = midpoint of class = (upper+lower)/2. FREQUENCY = count in that class.
steps.s: Class interval is the range. Class width is upper minus lower limit. Class mark is the midpoint. Frequency is the count.

steps.t: CUMULATIVE FREQUENCY = running total of frequencies. RELATIVE FREQUENCY = frequency/total × 100%. Exclusive class intervals (10-20, 20-30): 20 goes in 20-30 NOT 10-20. Upper limit is excluded!
steps.s: Cumulative frequency is the running total. In exclusive intervals, the upper limit belongs to the next class.

explain_questions.confirm_question: Class mark of the interval 20-30 is ____.
explain_questions.confirm_question_spoken: The class mark of the interval 20 to 30 is blank.
nudges: Class mark = (upper limit + lower limit) / 2
Answer: 25
explain_questions.anim_svg: <g id="st2s0" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="30" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Frequency Distribution: group data into classes</text></g><g id="st2s1" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="70" font-size="11" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Class width=upper-lower | Class mark=(u+l)/2</text></g><g id="st2s2" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="110" font-size="11" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Exclusive: upper limit→next class | Cum.freq=running total</text></g><g id="st2ans" opacity="0"><rect x="40" y="148" width="320" height="24" rx="7" fill="#eef2eb" stroke="#6b4c2a" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="210" y="163" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Share Tech Mono" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#7a8c6e">Class mark = (20+30)/2 = 25</text></g>

explain_questions.question: What is a Histogram and how do you draw one?
explain_questions.question_spoken: What is a Histogram and how do you draw one?

steps.t: A HISTOGRAM is a bar graph for continuous data (grouped frequency distribution). Key difference from bar graph: NO GAPS between bars! Bars touch each other because data is continuous.
steps.s: A histogram has no gaps between bars. It is used for continuous grouped data. This is the key difference from a bar graph.

steps.t: How to draw: X-axis = class intervals (plotted on a continuous scale). Y-axis = frequency. Draw bars with width = class width and height = frequency. Label both axes. Title the graph.
steps.s: X-axis shows class intervals. Y-axis shows frequency. Bars have width equal to class width and height equal to frequency.

steps.t: If class widths are UNEQUAL, use frequency density on Y-axis: Frequency Density = Frequency / Class Width. This ensures fair comparison between classes of different widths. Equal widths: frequency density = frequency!
steps.s: For unequal class widths, use frequency density equals frequency divided by class width on the y-axis.

explain_questions.confirm_question: In a histogram, the bars touch each other because data is ______.
explain_questions.confirm_question_spoken: In a histogram, the bars touch each other because data is blank.
nudges: Bar graphs have gaps for discrete data
Histograms have no gaps for what type of data?
Answer: Continuous (no gaps between continuous values)
explain_questions.anim_svg: <g id="st3s0" opacity="0"><rect x="10" y="8" width="390" height="30" rx="7" fill="#f5e6c8"/><text x="210" y="28" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#d4870a">Histogram: bars TOUCH (continuous data, no gaps!)</text></g><g id="st3s1" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="68" font-size="11" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">X: class intervals | Y: frequency | width=class width</text></g><g id="st3s2" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="108" font-size="11" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Unequal widths: use freq. density=freq/class width</text></g><g id="st3ans" opacity="0"><rect x="40" y="148" width="320" height="24" rx="7" fill="#eef2eb" stroke="#6b4c2a" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="210" y="163" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Share Tech Mono" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#7a8c6e">No gaps → Continuous data</text></g>

explain_questions.question: What is the Mean and how do you calculate it?
explain_questions.question_spoken: What is the Mean and how do you calculate it?

steps.t: MEAN (Arithmetic Mean) = Sum of all observations / Number of observations = Σx / n. It is the 'average' — the balance point of the data. Symbol: x̄ (x-bar).
steps.s: Mean equals sum of all values divided by number of values. It is the balance point of the data. Symbol is x bar.

steps.t: For UNGROUPED data: Mean = (x₁+x₂+...+xₙ)/n. Example: data 4,7,2,9,3. Mean = (4+7+2+9+3)/5 = 25/5 = 5. For GROUPED data (frequency table): Mean = Σ(fᵢxᵢ)/Σfᵢ where xᵢ is class mark.
steps.s: For ungrouped data, add all values and divide by count. For grouped data, multiply each class mark by its frequency, add, then divide by total frequency.

steps.t: ASSUMED MEAN METHOD: Choose a convenient assumed mean 'a'. Let dᵢ = xᵢ - a. Then Mean = a + (Σfᵢdᵢ/Σfᵢ). This reduces calculation! Used when class marks are large numbers.
steps.s: Assumed mean method: subtract assumed mean from each class mark, multiply by frequency, sum up, divide by total frequency, add assumed mean.

nudges: Mean = sum / count
explain_questions.anim_svg: <g id="st4s0" opacity="0"><rect x="10" y="8" width="390" height="30" rx="7" fill="#eef2eb"/><text x="210" y="28" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Mean = Σx/n (ungrouped) | Σfᵢxᵢ/Σfᵢ (grouped)</text></g><g id="st4s1" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="68" font-size="11" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">4,7,2,9,3: sum=25, n=5 → Mean=5</text></g><g id="st4s2" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="108" font-size="11" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Assumed mean: Mean=a+Σfᵢdᵢ/Σfᵢ where dᵢ=xᵢ-a</text></g><g id="st4ans" opacity="0"><rect x="40" y="148" width="320" height="24" rx="7" fill="#eef2eb" stroke="#6b4c2a" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="210" y="163" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Share Tech Mono" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#7a8c6e">Mean = (5+8+3+7+2)/5 = 25/5 = 5</text></g>

explain_questions.question: What is the Median and how do you find it?
explain_questions.question_spoken: What is the Median and how do you find it?

steps.t: MEDIAN = the middle value when data is arranged in ASCENDING ORDER. It divides the data into two equal halves. Not affected by extreme values (unlike mean)!
steps.s: Median is the middle value after arranging data in ascending order. It is not affected by extreme values.

steps.t: For ODD number of observations (n): Median = value at position (n+1)/2. Example: 2,3,5,7,9 (n=5). Position = (5+1)/2 = 3rd value = 5. For EVEN n: Median = average of (n/2)th and (n/2+1)th values.
steps.s: For odd n: median is the middle value at position n plus 1 over 2. For even n: average the two middle values.

steps.t: Example (even n): 2,4,5,7,8,9 (n=6). Middle positions: 3rd=5 and 4th=7. Median = (5+7)/2 = 6. Always SORT first! Many students forget to sort — always the first step.
steps.s: Even n: positions n over 2 and n over 2 plus 1. Average those two values. Always sort data first before finding median.

nudges: First arrange in order: 1, 3, 5, 7, 9
n=5 (odd). Position = (5+1)/2 = 3rd value
3rd value = 5. Median = 5
explain_questions.anim_svg: <g id="st5s0" opacity="0"><rect x="10" y="8" width="390" height="30" rx="7" fill="#f5e6c8"/><text x="210" y="28" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#d4870a">Median = middle value (SORT FIRST!)</text></g><g id="st5s1" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="68" font-size="11" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Odd n: pos=(n+1)/2 | Even n: avg of n/2 and (n/2+1)</text></g><g id="st5s2" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="108" font-size="11" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Even example: 2,4,5,7,8,9 → (5+7)/2=6</text></g><g id="st5ans" opacity="0"><rect x="40" y="148" width="320" height="24" rx="7" fill="#eef2eb" stroke="#6b4c2a" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="210" y="163" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Share Tech Mono" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#7a8c6e">1,3,5,7,9 → 3rd value = 5</text></g>

explain_questions.question: What is the Mode and how do you find it?
explain_questions.question_spoken: What is the Mode and how do you find it?

steps.t: MODE = the value that occurs MOST FREQUENTLY in the data. The most popular value! A data set can have: No mode (all values different), One mode (unimodal), Two modes (bimodal), Multiple modes.
steps.s: Mode is the value that appears most often. Data can have no mode, one mode, or multiple modes.

steps.t: Example: 4, 5, 3, 4, 7, 4, 2, 6. Count: 4 appears 3 times (most). Mode = 4. Example: 2, 3, 3, 4, 4, 5. Both 3 and 4 appear twice. Two modes: 3 and 4 (bimodal).
steps.s: The value appearing most is the mode. If two values tie, both are modes — the data is bimodal.

steps.t: Mode is most useful for: finding the most popular item (best-selling product, most common shoe size, most frequent score). Unlike mean and median, mode can be used for NON-NUMERICAL data too (most popular colour)!
steps.s: Mode is best for finding the most popular item. It works for non-numerical data too, unlike mean and median.

nudges: Count frequency of each value
2 appears 2 times, 3 appears 3 times, 4 appears 2 times, 5 appears 1 time
3 appears most often → Mode = 3
explain_questions.anim_svg: <g id="st6s0" opacity="0"><rect x="10" y="8" width="390" height="30" rx="7" fill="#eef2eb"/><text x="210" y="28" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Mode = most frequent value</text></g><g id="st6s1" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="68" font-size="11" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">4,5,3,4,7,4,2,6 → 4 appears 3 times → Mode=4</text></g><g id="st6s2" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="108" font-size="11" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Bimodal: 2 modes | Works for non-numerical data too!</text></g><g id="st6ans" opacity="0"><rect x="40" y="148" width="320" height="24" rx="7" fill="#eef2eb" stroke="#6b4c2a" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="210" y="163" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Share Tech Mono" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#7a8c6e">3 appears 3 times → Mode = 3</text></g>

explain_questions.question: What is a Frequency Polygon and how is it drawn?
explain_questions.question_spoken: What is a Frequency Polygon and how is it drawn?

steps.t: A FREQUENCY POLYGON is a line graph that shows the distribution of data. It is drawn by joining the midpoints (class marks) of the tops of histogram bars with straight lines. It is smoother than a histogram!
steps.s: A frequency polygon joins the midpoints of histogram bar tops with straight lines. It shows data distribution as a smooth shape.

steps.t: To draw WITHOUT histogram: Plot points (class mark, frequency) for each class. Add two extra points at zero frequency: one class before the first and one after the last. Join all points with straight lines.
steps.s: Plot each class mark against its frequency. Add zero-frequency points at each end. Join all points with straight lines.

steps.t: The polygon should be CLOSED — it starts and ends at the x-axis (zero frequency). The area under the frequency polygon equals the area of the histogram. Frequency polygons are useful to COMPARE two distributions on the same graph!
steps.s: The polygon closes at the x-axis. Its area equals the histogram area. Frequency polygons allow comparison of two datasets on the same graph.

explain_questions.confirm_question: A frequency polygon is drawn by joining midpoints of ______ of histogram bars.
explain_questions.confirm_question_spoken: A frequency polygon is drawn by joining the midpoints of blank of histogram bars.
nudges: Think about which part of each histogram bar is used
The highest point of each bar is...
Answer: Tops (the top edges of the bars)
explain_questions.anim_svg: <g id="st7s0" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="30" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Frequency Polygon: join midpoints of tops of bars</text></g><g id="st7s1" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="70" font-size="11" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Without histogram: plot (class mark, freq), add 0-freq ends</text></g><g id="st7s2" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="110" font-size="11" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Closed polygon | Same area as histogram | Compare 2 datasets</text></g><g id="st7ans" opacity="0"><rect x="40" y="148" width="320" height="24" rx="7" fill="#eef2eb" stroke="#6b4c2a" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="210" y="163" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Share Tech Mono" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#7a8c6e">Join midpoints of TOPS of histogram bars</text></g>

explain_questions.question: How do you compare Mean, Median and Mode?
explain_questions.question_spoken: How do you compare mean, median and mode?

steps.t: MEAN: Best when data is symmetric (no outliers). Uses ALL values. Affected by extreme values. Most mathematically useful. Example: average marks in class.
steps.s: Mean uses all values and is best for symmetric data. It is affected by outliers like a very high or very low value.

steps.t: MEDIAN: Best when data has outliers (extreme values). Divides data into two equal halves. Not affected by extremes. Example: median salary in a company (few very high salaries don't distort it).
steps.s: Median is best when there are outliers. It is not affected by extreme values. Best for salary or income data.

steps.t: MODE: Best for most popular item. Can be used for qualitative data. May not exist or may be multiple. Example: most common dress size, most popular product. Relationship: In symmetric distribution, Mean = Median = Mode!
steps.s: Mode is best for most popular item. In symmetric data, all three measures are equal. Mode works for non-numerical data.

explain_questions.confirm_question: Which measure of central tendency is NOT affected by extreme values?
explain_questions.confirm_question_spoken: Which measure of central tendency is not affected by extreme values?
nudges: Mean uses all values — so extreme values change it
Mode just finds the most frequent — but a single extreme value won't have high frequency
Median only cares about the middle position — extreme values don't change it
explain_questions.anim_svg: <g id="st8s0" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="30" font-size="11" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Mean: uses all | best for symmetric | affected by outliers</text></g><g id="st8s1" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="70" font-size="11" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Median: middle | NOT affected by outliers | best for salary</text></g><g id="st8s2" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="110" font-size="11" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Mode: most popular | works for qualitative data</text></g><g id="st8ans" opacity="0"><rect x="40" y="148" width="320" height="24" rx="7" fill="#eef2eb" stroke="#6b4c2a" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="210" y="163" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Share Tech Mono" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#7a8c6e">MEDIAN is not affected by extreme values</text></g>

explain_questions.question: How do you calculate Mean from a frequency distribution table?
explain_questions.question_spoken: How do you calculate mean from a frequency distribution table?

steps.t: For grouped data: Mean = Σ(fᵢ × xᵢ) / Σfᵢ. Where xᵢ = class mark of each class, fᵢ = frequency of each class. Step 1: Find class marks. Step 2: Multiply each class mark by frequency. Step 3: Sum all fᵢxᵢ. Step 4: Divide by Σfᵢ.
steps.s: Mean from frequency table: multiply each class mark by its frequency, sum all those products, divide by total frequency.

steps.t: Example: Classes 0-10, 10-20, 20-30 with frequencies 5, 8, 7. Class marks: 5, 15, 25. fᵢxᵢ: 25, 120, 175. Σfᵢxᵢ = 320. Σfᵢ = 20. Mean = 320/20 = 16.
steps.s: Classes 0-10, 10-20, 20-30 with frequencies 5, 8, 7. Class marks 5, 15, 25. Products: 25, 120, 175. Sum 320 divided by 20 equals mean 16.

steps.t: STEP DEVIATION METHOD: dᵢ = (xᵢ − a)/h where a = assumed mean, h = class width. Mean = a + h × (Σfᵢdᵢ/Σfᵢ). This is fastest when class width is constant. Very useful for CBSE board questions!
steps.s: Step deviation method: dᵢ equals class mark minus assumed mean divided by class width. Mean equals assumed mean plus class width times sum of f d over total frequency.

explain_questions.confirm_question: For classes 0-10, 10-20, 20-30 with frequencies 4, 6, 5 — what is Σfᵢ?
explain_questions.confirm_question_spoken: For classes 0-10, 10-20 and 20-30 with frequencies 4, 6 and 5 — what is the total frequency?
nudges: Σfᵢ means sum of all frequencies
Answer: Σfᵢ = 15
explain_questions.anim_svg: <g id="st9s0" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="30" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Grouped Mean = Σ(fᵢxᵢ)/Σfᵢ</text></g><g id="st9s1" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="70" font-size="11" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Classes:0-10,10-20,20-30 | f:5,8,7 | xᵢ:5,15,25</text></g><g id="st9s2" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="110" font-size="11" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Σfx=25+120+175=320 | Σf=20 | Mean=320/20=16</text></g><g id="st9ans" opacity="0"><rect x="40" y="148" width="320" height="24" rx="7" fill="#eef2eb" stroke="#6b4c2a" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="210" y="163" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Share Tech Mono" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#7a8c6e">Σfᵢ = 4+6+5 = 15</text></g>

explain_questions.question: What are the different types of graphical representations of data?
explain_questions.question_spoken: What are the different types of graphical representations of data?

steps.t: BAR GRAPH: for discrete data, bars have gaps. HISTOGRAM: for continuous grouped data, no gaps. FREQUENCY POLYGON: line graph connecting class mark vs frequency. PIE CHART: for parts of a whole (uses angles, 360° total).
steps.s: Bar graph for discrete data. Histogram for continuous data. Frequency polygon is a line version of histogram. Pie chart shows proportions.

steps.t: STEM-AND-LEAF PLOT: shows actual data values while grouped. Good for small datasets. Example: scores 34,36,45,47,52 → Stem 3: 4,6 | Stem 4: 5,7 | Stem 5: 2. Both stem and leaf are visible!
steps.s: Stem and leaf plot shows actual values grouped by first digits. Stem is tens digit, leaf is units digit.

steps.t: OGIVE (Cumulative Frequency Curve): graph of cumulative frequency vs upper class boundary. Shaped like an S-curve. Used to find median graphically (at 50th percentile). Less than ogive uses upper limits!
steps.s: Ogive is the cumulative frequency curve. It has an S-shape. The median can be read from the ogive at the 50th percentile.

explain_questions.confirm_question: Which graph is used for continuous grouped data with NO gaps between bars?
explain_questions.confirm_question_spoken: Which graph is used for continuous grouped data with no gaps between bars?
nudges: Bar graph has gaps. This one does NOT have gaps.
It is used for continuous grouped data
Answer: Histogram
explain_questions.anim_svg: <g id="st10s0" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="30" font-size="11" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Bar:gaps,discrete | Histogram:no gaps,continuous</text></g><g id="st10s1" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="70" font-size="11" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Freq. Polygon: line | Pie: proportions | Stem-Leaf: actual vals</text></g><g id="st10s2" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="110" font-size="11" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Ogive: cumulative freq curve (S-shape) → find median!</text></g><g id="st10ans" opacity="0"><rect x="40" y="148" width="320" height="24" rx="7" fill="#eef2eb" stroke="#6b4c2a" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="210" y="163" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Share Tech Mono" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#7a8c6e">Histogram — no gaps, continuous data</text></g>

practice_questions.question: Find the mean of: 12, 18, 15, 20, 25, 10
practice_questions.trick: Mean = sum/count. Add all values, divide by how many. Always count carefully!

practice_questions.question: Find the median of: 7, 3, 11, 5, 9, 1, 13
practice_questions.trick: SORT FIRST! Then for odd n, find the middle position (n+1)/2. Here 4th value is 7.

practice_questions.question: Find the mode of: 4, 7, 4, 9, 3, 7, 4, 7, 3
steps: Count: 4 appears 3 times, 7 appears 3 times, 3 appears 2 times, 9 appears 1 time
Both 4 and 7 appear equally most often (3 times each)
practice_questions.trick: Count frequencies carefully! When two values tie for most frequent, both are modes. This is called bimodal data.
practice_questions.alt: Tally: 4 three times, 7 three times. Tie! Both 4 and 7 are modes.

practice_questions.question: Class mark of the interval 30-40 is ____.
practice_questions.question_spoken: The class mark of the interval 30 to 40 is blank.
practice_questions.trick: Class mark = midpoint = (lower+upper)/2. Always the average of the two class boundaries!

practice_questions.question: Find the median of: 15, 22, 8, 17, 31, 11, 25, 20
practice_questions.trick: Even n: average of (n/2)th and (n/2+1)th. Here n=8: 4th and 5th values. (17+20)/2=18.5.

practice_questions.question: In a histogram, the class width is 10 and frequency is 15. What is the frequency density?
practice_questions.question_spoken: In a histogram, class width is 10 and frequency is 15. What is the frequency density?
practice_questions.trick: Frequency density = frequency/class width. Used when class widths are unequal for fair comparison.
practice_questions.alt: Divide frequency by class width: 15/10=1.5. This normalises for class width differences.

practice_questions.question: The mean of 5 numbers is 18. If one number is removed and the mean becomes 16, what was the removed number?
practice_questions.question_spoken: The mean of 5 numbers is 18. If one number is removed and the mean becomes 16, what was the removed number?
practice_questions.trick: Sum = mean × count. Find original sum, find new sum, subtract to get the removed value. Classic CBSE type!
practice_questions.alt: Original total=90. After removal, 4 numbers with mean 16: total=64. Removed=90-64=26.

practice_questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a measure of central tendency? A: Mean  B: Range  C: Median  D: Mode
practice_questions.question_spoken: Which is NOT a measure of central tendency? A: Mean. B: Range. C: Median. D: Mode.
steps: Mean, Median and Mode are the three measures of central tendency
Range = Maximum − Minimum → measures SPREAD not centre
Range is a measure of dispersion, not central tendency
practice_questions.trick: Central tendency = centre of data (Mean, Median, Mode). Range = spread = max minus min. Range is about how spread out data is!
practice_questions.alt: Range tells how wide the data is. Mean, median, mode tell where the data is centred. Range is a measure of spread.

practice_questions.question: Find the mean of data: values 10, 20, 30 with frequencies 4, 5, 6 respectively.
practice_questions.question_spoken: Find the mean of data with values 10, 20, 30 having frequencies 4, 5 and 6.
practice_questions.trick: Grouped mean = Σfx/Σf. Multiply each value by its frequency, sum up, divide by total frequency.

practice_questions.question: If the mean of 6, 8, x, 14, 16 is 11, find x.
practice_questions.question_spoken: If the mean of 6, 8, x, 14 and 16 is 11, find x.
practice_questions.trick: Mean × count = sum. So sum = 11×5=55. Known values sum = 6+8+14+16=44. x=55-44=11.

practice_questions.question: How many total observations are there if frequencies are 8, 12, 15, 10, 5?
practice_questions.question_spoken: How many total observations are there if frequencies are 8, 12, 15, 10 and 5?
steps: Total observations = sum of all frequencies
practice_questions.trick: Total observations = Σf = sum of all frequencies. Simple addition!

practice_questions.question: The median of 2, 5, 7, 9, 11 is ____.
practice_questions.question_spoken: The median of 2, 5, 7, 9 and 11 is blank.
practice_questions.trick: Data is already sorted here! n=5 odd, position=(5+1)/2=3. Third value is 7.
practice_questions.alt: Middle value of 5 items is the 3rd one: 2,5,7→ median=7.

practice_questions.question: In a class, scores are: 85, 92, 78, 92, 88, 75, 92, 80. Find the mode.
practice_questions.question_spoken: Scores are 85, 92, 78, 92, 88, 75, 92 and 80. Find the mode.
practice_questions.trick: Mode = most frequent. Here 92 appears 3 times while all others appear once. Easy!
practice_questions.alt: Tally: 92 occurs at positions 2, 4, 7 — three times. Most frequent = mode = 92.

practice_questions.question: The class width of the interval 25-35 is ____.
practice_questions.question_spoken: The class width of the interval 25 to 35 is blank.
steps: Class width = upper limit − lower limit
practice_questions.trick: Class width = difference between upper and lower class limits. Simple subtraction!
practice_questions.alt: 35 minus 25 equals 10. The interval spans 10 units.

practice_questions.question: Mean marks of 30 students is 60. 10 more students with mean 40 join. Find new mean.
practice_questions.question_spoken: Mean marks of 30 students is 60. 10 more students with mean 40 join. Find the new mean.
practice_questions.trick: Combined mean: total sum / total count. Don't average the means directly — that would be wrong! Always use actual sums.
practice_questions.alt: Total sum=1800+400=2200. Total count=40. New mean=2200/40=55.
